core_intuitions: Exponent bits buy dynamic range; mantissa bits buy precision. The bit-budget split IS the whole trade, and for training range beats precision — which is why bf16 (fp32-sized exponent, 7-bit mantissa) beats fp16 (5-bit exponent, 10-bit mantissa) despite equal size.

Tensor cores take narrow inputs but accumulate the long dot-product reduction in fp32 — 'narrow inputs, wide accumulator' — which is the single trick that makes any low-precision matmul numerically survivable.

fp16 has TWO distinct failures with TWO distinct fixes: gradient underflow (small grads round to 0), fixed by loss scaling; and update swamping/stagnation (a tiny update added to a large weight is lost below half an ulp), fixed by keeping fp32 master weights.

Loss scaling multiplies the loss by S so the chain rule lifts the entire gradient distribution up out of the underflow zone; it is a mathematical no-op on the final update. bf16 needs none because its exponent already matches fp32.

FP8's 3-bit mantissa (~12.5% spacing) forces scaling to map each tensor into the tiny representable window: per-tensor scaling, then blockwise scaling to stop a single outlier from crushing every value; delayed scaling hides the amax reduction using a rolling history.
FP8 is for the big GEMMs only; large-range reductions and divisions by small numbers (softmax, norms, loss/log-softmax, optimizer state, master weights) stay in fp32.

existing.name: fp8mp-tensorcore-accumulate
existing.issue: Conveys 'narrow inputs, wide fp32 accumulator' well in light theme, but in dark theme the pale-blue 'multiply in bf16 / a_ik x b_kj' box renders dark text on a dark fill (barely legible) and the grey bottom track labels ('16-bit', '32-bit wide accumulator') fade out. The idea survives but the middle stage and the labeling of the pipeline stages lose contrast.
existing.improvement: Make the middle 'multiply in bf16' box and the bottom stage-track labels theme-aware (use currentColor / token-based fill+stroke so text stays legible on both backgrounds). Keep the hardcoded dark accumulator box (it works in both) but verify its 'sum of 4096 partial products' subtext contrast. Optionally reinforce the core message with a one-line caption 'inputs narrow (16/8-bit), the error-accumulating reduction stays 32-bit'.

existing.name: loss-scaling
existing.improvement: No change needed. The 'gradient distribution shifts up out of the fp16 underflow band into the representable window' visual plus the L x S -> backward -> unscale -> step-fp32 pipeline strip is exactly the right intuition and reads cleanly in both themes.

existing.name: fp8mp-pertensor-vs-blockwise-scaling
existing.issue: Excellent side-by-side (one outlier poisons the whole tensor vs. one outlier contained to its 128x128 tile) in light theme. In dark theme the per-tile subscript labels (S00..S33) sit as dark text on the tile fills and the small multi-line explanatory captions under each panel drop to low contrast, so the payoff text ('other 15 tiles keep full FP8 resolution') is hard to read.
existing.improvement: Bump the tile subscript labels and the small explanatory captions to a theme-aware high-contrast token in dark mode; ensure the green 'contained' tiles keep legible label text. No structural change — the metaphor and the FP8-codes ruler on the left are strong.

new_figures.name: fp8mp-format-anatomy-range-vs-precision
new_figures.concept: Bit-field anatomy of fp32/fp16/bf16/fp8 side by side, showing how the sign|exponent|mantissa split sets range vs precision.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's own thesis: 'the split between exponent bits and mantissa bits is the entire story of mixed precision' and 'read this table as the key to everything that follows.' Yet no figure shows the bit layout. This is THE foundational visual — every later idea (bf16 needs no loss scaling, fp16 underflows, FP8 needs scaling, E4M3 vs E5M2) is a corollary of this one picture. A reader who internalizes 'exponent = range meter, mantissa = precision meter, and the total width is fixed' understands the whole chapter.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The floating-point formats: fp32, fp16, bf16, fp8', immediately after the format table and before the '### Tensor cores accumulate in fp32' subsection.
new_figures.brief: A single static infographic. Draw five horizontal bit-field bars, one per format, left-aligned and stacked top to bottom in this order: FP32, FP16, BF16, FP8 E4M3, FP8 E5M2. Each bar is segmented left-to-right into three regions: a thin SIGN cell (1 bit), an EXPONENT region, and a MANTISSA region, with segment WIDTHS drawn proportional to the bit counts so the eye reads the trade geometrically. Label each region with its bit count: FP32 = 1/8/23, FP16 = 1/5/10, BF16 = 1/8/7, E4M3 = 1/4/3, E5M2 = 1/5/2. Use ONE consistent visual treatment for all exponent regions and a DIFFERENT consistent one for all mantissa regions (theme-safe shading/hatching, not hardcoded brand colors), and add a legend: 'exponent bits -> dynamic RANGE (how big / how small)' and 'mantissa bits -> PRECISION (how finely you can tell nearby numbers apart)'. Critical alignment device: draw the FP32 bar as the reference at the top; place the BF16 bar directly beneath it and align their exponent regions edge-to-edge, then draw a dashed 'chopped here' cut line showing bf16 = fp32 with the bottom 16 mantissa bits removed (label: 'bf16 = fp32 with the mantissa tail cut off -> same range, coarser precision'). Put a subtle bracket connecting FP16 and BF16 with the caption 'same 16 bits, opposite trade: fp16 buys precision, bf16 buys range -> for training, range wins'. To the RIGHT of each bar, show two tiny horizontal meter bars, a RANGE meter and a PRECISION meter, filled proportionally so the pattern is visible at a glance: fp32 = long range + long precision; fp16 = short range + medium precision; bf16 = long range + short precision; e4m3 = very short range + tiny precision; e5m2 = slightly longer range + tinier precision. Optionally annotate each exponent region with its max finite value drawn from the chapter table only (fp16 -> 65504, e4m3 -> 448, e5m2 -> 57344) as small tags on the range meter; do NOT invent any other numbers. Everything must fit the viewBox with no overlaps and a clear top-to-bottom reading order; both themes must keep all bit-count labels and legends legible.

new_figures.name: fp8mp-master-weights-swamping
new_figures.concept: Update swamping (stagnation): a tiny weight update lands below half an fp16 ulp and rounds back to the old weight, so it is silently lost — fixed by applying the update to an fp32 master copy.
new_figures.rationale: The 'why naive fp16 breaks' section names two DISTINCT failures with two DISTINCT fixes. Loss scaling (underflow) already has a figure; update swamping does NOT, yet the chapter treats it as equally foundational ('the update is silently lost... this is the stagnation problem'), ties it to master weights, Kahan summation, memory accounting, and stochastic rounding, and returns to it in the Key Takeaways. Readers routinely conflate the two problems; a number-line figure that separates 'grad too small to represent' from 'update too small to STICK when added to a big weight' is the clarifying visual the chapter needs.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Why naive fp16 breaks: range, underflow, and the update problem', after the worked w = 1.0 / eta*g = 2e-4 example paragraph and before the numbered '1. Master weights in fp32 / 2. Loss scaling' list.
new_figures.brief: A static two-panel comparison built around number lines, using the chapter's own worked example: weight w = 1.0, desired update eta*g = 2e-4, target 0.9998. TOP panel titled 'fp16 update: swamped': draw a horizontal number line zoomed around 1.0 with fp16 grid ticks spaced one ulp apart (label the spacing '1 ulp ~ 2^-10 ~ 9.77e-4'); mark w = 1.0 sitting exactly on a tick. Draw a short leftward arrow of length 2e-4 from 1.0 (label 'w - eta*g = 0.9998 wanted'); show that its landing point falls INSIDE the first grid cell, less than half a tick from 1.0, and a 'round-to-nearest' snap arrow bends it back onto the 1.0 tick. Big verdict tag: 'update < half an ulp -> rounds back to 1.0 -> LOST'. Add a small strip beside it showing 'after many steps' with the value stuck flat at 1.0 (stagnation). BOTTOM panel titled 'fp32 master: sticks': same zoom, but a FINE fp32 grid (many closely spaced ticks) where 0.9998 lands on a real representable tick; the update arrow ends cleanly on it. Verdict tag: 'representable -> update accumulates -> progress'. Connect the two panels with a small loop diagram of the standard recipe: 'compute in fp16 (fast)' -> 'tiny gradient' -> 'apply update to fp32 MASTER weight (precise)' -> 'cast fresh fp16 copy for next forward' -> back to compute; annotate that the optimizer touches only the fp32 master. Keep a one-line footer distinguishing this from loss scaling: 'loss scaling fixes grads that are too small to REPRESENT; master weights fix updates too small to STICK.' Use only the small illustrative numbers already in the chapter (1.0, 2e-4, 0.9998, 2^-10 spacing); invent nothing else. Number lines, ticks, arrows, and verdict tags must stay legible and non-overlapping in both light and dark themes (theme-aware strokes/text, no hardcoded brand colors).

summary: The chapter is concept-rich and already has three figures, one of which (loss-scaling) is excellent and two of which (tensorcore-accumulate, pertensor-vs-blockwise) are solid but have dark-theme contrast bugs on their secondary text/labels — refine, not replace. The two genuinely foundational gaps are both flagged by the chapter's own emphasis: (1) the sign/exponent/mantissa bit-anatomy that the text calls 'the entire story' and 'the key to everything' but never draws, and (2) the update-swamping / fp32-master-weights intuition, the second of the two distinct fp16 failure modes, whose sibling (underflow/loss-scaling) has a figure while it has none. Both are best as crisp static infographics — bit-field bars with range/precision meters, and a number-line half-ulp-landing comparison — since a single frame conveys each; no long-animation is warranted here.
